one_line_takeaway: NSPU shows that a lightweight, PCA-derived linear projection filter applied only at the model's final hidden layer -- driven by activations estimated from anonymized (PII-masked) text via a learned neuro-semantic aligner -- can outperform gradient-based LLM unlearning baselines (GA, GD, KLM, DPO, NPO) on a combined forgetting/utility tradeoff score while never exposing the raw forget set to the model operator and using roughly 10x fewer FLOPs, though the approach is an empirical approximation with no formal unlearning or inversion-resistance guarantees and requires white-box activation access.
key_finding: On the Aggregate score (sum of HPS+CES+HRS+HCNLL, Table 1), NSPU outperforms all five baselines on every one of the four tested models, e.g. Llama2-7B: NSPU 4.073 vs. best baseline GD 1.765 and worst GA -0.748; Mistral-7B: NSPU 7.849 vs. best baseline NPO 1.406; OLMoE: NSPU 8.669 vs. best baseline NPO 4.837; Llama-13B: NSPU 4.882 vs. best baseline GD 3.088. GA and KLM produce catastrophic utility loss (large negative CES/Aggregate, e.g. Llama2-7B GA Aggregate -0.748) despite high forget-side perplexity gain, because they only maximize forget-set loss with no retention mechanism. On computational cost (Table 2), NSPU requires roughly an order of magnitude fewer FLOPs than the gradient-based baselines (e.g. on LLaMA-7B: NSPU 7.6x10^16 FLOPs vs. GA/KLM 1.3x10^17 and GD/DPO/NPO 5.2x10^17) and is stated in the abstract/intro to be 'at least 10x more computationally efficient than standard unlearning methods' and ~10^6x more efficient than retraining from scratch. On MIA-resistance (Separation Quality Score, Table 2), NSPU has the highest average SQS across the four models (0.77) versus GA/KLM (0.68), GD (0.66), DPO (0.59), NPO (0.56). Entity-level ablation (Table 4, Llama-13B) shows NSPU-estimated activations achieve 0.80-0.85 similarity to true original activations across PII types (PERSON 0.85, LOCATION 0.84, PHONE_NUMBER 0.84, DATE_TIME 0.80, Misc 0.83), and the paper reports NSPU 'consistently erases targeted representations 83% of the time on average' in the entity-level analysis. Layer-wise analysis (Section 5.1) shows activations are identical between original and unlearned models up through layer 31, with separation appearing only at the final (32nd) hidden layer where the unlearning filter is applied, which the authors argue limits exposure of sensitive representations to attackers probing intermediate layers.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold: (1) it defines a new task formulation called 'Shadow Unlearning', where an LLM unlearning method is given only an anonymized (NER-masked) version of the forget set rather than the raw PII-bearing forget set, so the model operator never sees the sensitive data; and (2) it proposes Neuro-Semantic Projector Unlearning (NSPU), a three-stage method that operates under this stricter setting: (i) a lightweight frozen-target MLP 'Latent Representation Aligner' Pθ is trained (on a separate public corpus of paired original/anonymized text) to map activations of anonymized text to the activations the original text would have produced at a chosen layer, with an added 'Inversion Optimization Score' (InvOptScore) regularization term that penalizes projected activations that are easy to invert back to plausible input embeddings; (ii) the trained aligner is applied to the anonymized forget set's activations to estimate original-space activations, which are stacked and PCA-decomposed to obtain a low-rank 'forget subspace' basis U; (iii) a non-trainable linear 'unlearning filter' ULfilter = I - alphaUU^T is spliced into the model's final layer normalization at inference time, projecting the last hidden state away from the forget subspace. The authors also construct a new benchmark, MuFU (Multi-domain Fictitious Unlearning: 4,000 synthetic PII-bearing samples across 5 domains -- Digital Informatics, Finance, Sports, Science & Technology, Politics -- generated with Gemini 3 Pro), because they argue the standard TOFU benchmark has excessive forget/retain semantic overlap that confounds unlearning evaluation. They further propose 4 new tradeoff-aware evaluation metrics (Harmonic Perplexity Score, Combined Efficacy Score, Harmonic ROUGE Score, Harmonic Conditional NLL) that harmonically combine a 'forget' term and a 'retain' term into one aggregate score.
threat_model: The paper's threat model is a privacy/operational one, not an adversarial-attacker one in the classic sense, though it does invoke membership-inference attacks (MIAs) as an evaluation/adversarial lens. Setting: a model operator (e.g., an MLaaS provider) must unlearn a forget set on behalf of a data owner (e.g., in healthcare or finance), but transmitting the raw forget set to the operator for unlearning risks data breaches and membership-inference exposure. The paper's stated privacy boundary is maintained at the data-owner side: the owner anonymizes PII locally (via Microsoft Presidio NER-based anonymization, replacing entities like <PERSON>, <LOCATION> with tags) and only the anonymized forget set ever leaves the owner's environment. NSPU's own Latent Representation Aligner is explicitly checked for a secondary privacy risk it could introduce: an adversary (assumed to have white-box access to the aligner's estimated activations) attempting optimization-based inversion of the projected activation back into a plausible input embedding. This is measured, not defended against with a formal guarantee, via InvOptScore, and the authors explicitly disclose in Limitations that they provide 'no formal guarantees against advanced white-box inversion attacks' on early/unmodified layers. Separately, the paper evaluates unlearning success using membership-inference attacks (Shokri et al. framing) as an evaluator: an adversary tries to distinguish forget-set samples from held-out non-member samples using per-sample CLM loss; the paper's own Separation Quality Score (SQS) quantifies MIA resistance of the unlearned model.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations (Section 7): NSPU's forget subspace is built via PCA, which captures directions of maximum variance rather than exact causal influence on model behavior, so it is an approximation. The pipeline is designed for batch unlearning; behavior under extremely low-resource settings (e.g., a single forget sample) is unexplored, and sequential/continuous unlearning requests without cumulative utility degradation remain an open problem. The unlearning filter is applied only at the final hidden layer, leaving intermediate-layer activations structurally unchanged; the authors state they provide no formal guarantee against advanced white-box inversion attacks targeting those unmodified earlier layers, only an empirical/interpretability argument. There is no theoretical guarantee that NSPU's activation-space projection is equivalent to retraining from scratch without the forget data (i.e., it is not certified/exact unlearning). NSPU requires access to internal model activations, which restricts its applicability to open-source (white-box) models and excludes closed API-only models. Finally, the NER-based Presidio anonymization pipeline can map distinct original samples to identical anonymized text (collisions), which can confuse the Latent Representation Aligner and cause unintended retention or forgetting when only a subset of colliding samples should be unlearned.

datasets_used: MuFU (Multi-domain Fictitious Unlearning): 4,000 synthetic samples, 5 domains (Digital Informatics, Finance, Sports, Science & Technology, Politics), 800 samples/domain, generated with Gemini 3 Pro in TOFU-style fictitious-fact format
TOFU (Maini et al., 2024a) -- used as the 0% baseline variant and blended with MuFU in 50%/100% heterogeneity variants
A separate public corpus of six combined PII datasets used only to pretrain the Latent Representation Aligner (Appendix C, Table 9)
quantitative_results: Aggregate score (Table 1): NSPU 4.073 (Llama2-7B), 7.849 (Mistral-7B), 8.669 (OLMoE), 4.882 (Llama-13B), higher than every baseline on every model
Best baseline Aggregate scores: GD 1.765 (Llama2-7B), NPO 1.406 (Mistral-7B), NPO 4.837 (OLMoE), GD 3.088 (Llama-13B)
GA and KLM produce negative Aggregate scores (catastrophic utility loss), e.g. GA -0.748 and KLM -0.751 on Llama2-7B
FLOPs (Table 2, LLaMA-7B): NSPU 7.6x10^16 vs GA/KLM 1.3x10^17 vs GD/DPO/NPO 5.2x10^17 vs full retraining 8.4x10^22
Abstract-stated efficiency: NSPU at least 10x more computationally efficient than standard unlearning methods and ~10^6x more efficient than retraining from scratch
Separation Quality Score average across 4 models (Table 2): NSPU 0.77, GA/KLM 0.68, GD 0.66, DPO 0.59, NPO 0.56
Domain-wise activation reconstruction similarity (Table 3, average per model): Llama-7B 0.71, Mistral-7B 0.59, OLMoE 0.68, Llama-13B 0.84
PII attribute-wise similarity on Llama-13B (Table 4): PERSON 0.85, LOCATION 0.84, PHONE_NUMBER 0.84, DATE_TIME 0.80, Misc 0.83
Entity-level analysis: NSPU consistently erases targeted representations 83% of the time on average (Section 5.2)
baselines_compared: Full retraining from scratch (compared only on FLOPs, Table 2)
